title: The Last Signal
excerpt: A radio operator picks up a transmission from a station that shut down twenty years ago.

featuredImage.alt: An abandoned radio relay station beneath storm clouds at dusk

author.name: Elena Voss
author.bio: Elena writes atmospheric mysteries where silence speaks louder than confession. Her work has appeared in literary journals across Europe.

content: The rain had stopped by the time Mara reached the old relay station, but the silence that followed felt heavier than any storm. She adjusted her headset and listened — not for static, not for voices, but for the impossible rhythm she had heard three nights in a row.
Every transmission began the same way: a single breath, a pause, and then her own name spoken in a voice she recognized from a photograph kept in a drawer she never opened.

Station KRX-7 had been decommissioned in 2006. Mara knew this because she had signed the paperwork herself, standing in this same room while technicians stripped the equipment and sealed the doors. The building was supposed to be empty. The frequency was supposed to be dead.
She checked the console. Signal strength: 94%. Source: internal. The needle did not waver.

The voice returned at 2:17 a.m., clearer than before. It read coordinates — latitude and longitude that pointed to a valley two hours north, a place that no longer appeared on any map she owned. Then it said her mother's name, and Mara understood the transmission was not a message from the past.
It was an invitation.

title: Midnight Garden
excerpt: Every night at twelve, flowers bloom in a courtyard no one remembers planting.

featuredImage.alt: Moonlit courtyard filled with luminous flowers in full bloom

author.name: Jonah Reed
author.bio: Jonah crafts worlds rooted in folklore and wonder. He believes the best fantasy feels like a memory you never had.

content: The courtyard had been paved over for decades — or so the building records claimed. Yet every night at twelve, the stones cracked open like eggshells, and flowers pushed through in colors that did not exist in any botanical guide Theo had ever read.

He first noticed them from his third-floor window: blue petals that glowed from within, stems that moved without wind. By the second week, he was waiting downstairs with a notebook, recording what bloomed and when.

On the fourteenth night, a single white flower appeared at the center of the courtyard. Its petals were shaped like hands, open and reaching. Theo stepped closer and heard humming — not from the flower, but from beneath the stones, as if something vast and patient was waking below.
He did not pick the flower. He wrote its name in his notebook using letters he did not remember learning, and when he looked up, the courtyard was empty again — paved, ordinary, and quiet.
The next morning, his notebook held a pressed petal he had never placed there, and a sentence in his own handwriting: Come back tonight.

title: Letters Never Sent
excerpt: Two strangers find a box of unsent letters in the attic of a shared apartment building.

featuredImage.alt: A wooden box of aged letters tied with faded ribbon in a sunlit attic

author.name: Amara Singh
author.bio: Amara explores love in its quietest forms — missed connections, second chances, and the courage it takes to begin again.

content: The attic door stuck, as attic doors do in old buildings where no one is meant to go. Priya found the box behind a stack of water-stained textbooks. Inside: forty-three letters, all addressed to the same person, none of them signed.
Downstairs, her neighbor Daniel was moving in — boxes labeled kitchen, books, fragile. They met in the hallway when Priya descended with the wooden crate under her arm.
You found those too, Daniel said. It was not a question.

The letters spanned thirty years. They described a life lived in parallel — two people in the same building, passing on the stairs, sharing a laundry room, never speaking beyond hello. The writer had loved in silence, recording every almost-moment in careful prose.

Priya and Daniel read them together over three evenings, sitting on the stairwell where the original writer and recipient had once passed each other daily. On the last page of the final letter, a line had been added in different ink: Some stories wait for new readers.
Daniel looked at Priya. Priya looked at the empty space where a signature should have been. They did not need the letters to tell them what came next.

title: Static on Channel Seven
excerpt: A late-night broadcast interrupts with footage from inside viewers' own homes.

featuredImage.alt: A television screen filled with static and a faint silhouette

author.name: Marcus Hale
author.bio: Marcus specializes in slow-burn dread and stories that linger long after the screen goes dark.

content: Channel Seven had been off the air since 1998. That did not stop it from appearing on Ren's television at 1:04 a.m., snowing with static until a picture resolved — his kitchen, filmed from the hallway he was not standing in.
He checked the cable box. Disconnected. He checked the window. Locked. The feed continued: his hand reaching for the remote, delayed by three seconds, as if the broadcast were watching him watch it.
Ren called the station. The number rang once and connected to silence, then to breathing, then to his own voice saying turn around.
He did not turn around. He counted to ten and looked at the screen instead. The camera angle had changed. It was now filming from inside his bedroom, where he had not been all evening.
On the bed, the sheets were moving. On the television, Ren saw himself walk into frame — not from behind, but from the direction of the front door, which he had deadbolted hours ago.

The broadcast ended at 1:17 a.m. The guide listed the program as Static on Channel Seven, episode one. Ren searched for episode two and found nothing. When he finally slept, the television turned itself on again, quietly, to an empty room.

title: The Orbit of Small Things
excerpt: An engineer aboard a deep-space relay learns the station has been drifting off course for months.

featuredImage.alt: A deep-space relay station drifting against a field of distant stars

author.name: Dr. Lena Okonkwo
author.bio: Lena writes speculative fiction at the intersection of science, isolation, and what it means to be human far from home.

content: The correction burn should have taken four minutes. Instead, the station's engines fired for eleven, and when they stopped, Dr. Sera Okonkwo checked the telemetry three times before accepting what it showed: Kepler Relay had been drifting off course for one hundred and forty-seven days.
Mission control had not noticed. Or had not reported it. The logs showed regular check-ins, green statuses, routine maintenance. Someone had been lying, or something had been intercepting the data.

Sera ran diagnostics alone. The station hummed around her — recycled air, artificial gravity, the familiar percussion of machinery keeping her alive six light-hours from Earth. Outside the viewport, stars did not move. That was the problem. They should have.

In the communications array, she found a repeating signal buried beneath the official feed: coordinates, timestamps, and a single word in plain text — return. The signal was not coming from Earth. It was coming from ahead, from the direction the station had been drifting toward without anyone on board knowing.

Sera plotted the trajectory. At current drift, Kepler Relay would reach the signal source in eleven days. Mission protocol required her to report and wait for instruction. But the logs had been falsified for five months, and waiting had already cost her the truth.

She opened a new channel and recorded a message for whoever was still listening back home. Then she set a course correction — not toward Earth, but toward the signal, toward whatever had been pulling the station like a tide.
Small things, she thought, have orbits too. Even loneliness. Even lies. Even a body alone in the dark, deciding finally to move.

title: After the Applause
excerpt: A retired performer returns to the stage one final time and discovers the audience already knows the ending.

featuredImage.alt: An empty theater stage lit by a single warm spotlight

author.name: Claire Whitfield
author.bio: Claire tells intimate stories about ambition, regret, and the performances we give even when the curtain falls.

content: The theater had been closed for two years when Vivian received the invitation to return. One night only. No recording. No press. The note did not say who was hosting, only that the audience would be ready.

She had not performed since the diagnosis — since the tremor in her left hand became impossible to hide and the reviews turned from praise to elegy. But the stage was the only place where her body still made sense.

On the night of the performance, the house was full. Faces in the dark, attentive and still. Vivian began her monologue — a piece she had written twenty years ago about a woman who keeps performing after everyone has stopped watching.

At the line where the character confesses her fear of being forgotten, the audience spoke with her, word for word, as if they had rehearsed it. Vivian faltered, then continued. They knew every pause, every breath, every beat she had ever invented.
When the monologue ended, the applause was immediate and overwhelming. Vivian bowed. In the front row, she saw her younger self, nineteen years old, clapping with tears on her face.

After the applause faded, the theater emptied quickly. Vivian stood alone on stage and understood: the audience had not come to watch her perform. They had come to help her remember that she already had.
She turned off the spotlight herself and walked into the wings, lighter than she had felt in years.
